examples.explanation: Step 1: Call isMatch('aa', 'a'). p is not empty, first_match = (s not empty and p[0] == s[0]) = ('a' == 'a') = true. len(p) >=2 and p[1]=='*'? No (p='a'). So, return first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p[1:]) = true and isMatch('a', ''). Step 2: Call isMatch('a', ''). p is empty, s is not empty ('a'), so return false. Step 3: Back to step 1: true and false = false. Final output: false.

Step 1: Call isMatch('aa', 'a*'). p not empty, first_match = (s not empty and p[0] in {s[0], '.'}) = ('a' in {'a'}) = true. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes (p='a*'). Branch: Option A (zero match): isMatch(s, p[2:]) = isMatch('aa', '') -> p empty, s not empty, return false. Option B (one-or-more match): first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p) = true and isMatch('a', 'a*'). Step 2: Call isMatch('a', 'a*'). first_match = ('a' in {'a'}) = true. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes. Branch: Option A: isMatch('a', '') -> false. Option B: true and isMatch('', 'a*'). Step 3: Call isMatch('', 'a*'). first_match = (s empty) = false. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes. Branch: Option A: isMatch('', '') -> true (both empty). Option B: false and isMatch(...) -> false. So isMatch('', 'a*') = true. Step 4: Back to step 2: Option B = true and true = true. So isMatch('a', 'a*') = true. Step 5: Back to step 1: Option B = true and true = true. Since Option A was false, final result = true. Output: true.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(n+m))
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible matches by handling wildcards like '*' via branching. Use it as a baseline to understand the problem's recursive nature.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Regex Matching
ladders.desc: Implement regex matching with '.' and '*' using recursion to explore all possible matches.
ladders.explanation: This problem involves matching an input string 's' against a pattern 'p' containing '.' (matches any single character) and '*' (matches zero or more of the preceding element). The brute force recursion approach explores all possible ways the pattern can match the string by recursively branching on wildcard cases, particularly '*', which requires trying both zero and one-or-more matches. The intuition is to treat the pattern as a sequence of characters with special rules, and recursively check if the remaining string matches the remaining pattern after handling each character. At each step, we compare the first characters of 's' and 'p': if they match (directly or via '.'), we proceed; if '*' is encountered, we branch into two possibilities: skip the '*' and its preceding character (match zero times) or match one character and keep the '*' for potential further matches. This exhaustive search ensures correctness but is inefficient due to exponential time complexity from branching. The algorithm uses recursion with base cases: if pattern is empty, return whether string is empty; if pattern has length >=2 and second character is '*', handle branching; else, check first character match and recurse. Time complexity is O(2^(n+m)) in worst case due to branching, and space is O(n+m) for recursion stack. This serves as a baseline to understand the problem's recursive structure before optimizing with memoization or DP. Common mistakes include not handling '*' correctly (e.g., forgetting zero-match case) or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def isMatch(s, p): if not p: return not s; first_match = bool(s) and p[0] in {s[0], '.'}; if len(p) >= 2 and p[1] == '*': return isMatch(s, p[2:]) or (first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p)); else: return first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p[1:]). Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of handling '*' with branching, which is the core of the recursion.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call isMatch('aa', 'a'). p is not empty, first_match = (s not empty and p[0] == s[0]) = ('a' == 'a') = true. len(p) >=2 and p[1]=='*'? No (p='a'). So, return first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p[1:]) = true and isMatch('a', ''). Step 2: Call isMatch('a', ''). p is empty, s is not empty ('a'), so return false. Step 3: Back to step 1: true and false = false. Final output: false.

Step 1: Call isMatch('aa', 'a*'). p not empty, first_match = (s not empty and p[0] in {s[0], '.'}) = ('a' in {'a'}) = true. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes (p='a*'). Branch: Option A (zero match): isMatch(s, p[2:]) = isMatch('aa', '') -> p empty, s not empty, return false. Option B (one-or-more match): first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p) = true and isMatch('a', 'a*'). Step 2: Call isMatch('a', 'a*'). first_match = ('a' in {'a'}) = true. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes. Branch: Option A: isMatch('a', '') -> false. Option B: true and isMatch('', 'a*'). Step 3: Call isMatch('', 'a*'). first_match = (s empty) = false. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes. Branch: Option A: isMatch('', '') -> true (both empty). Option B: false and isMatch(...) -> false. So isMatch('', 'a*') = true. Step 4: Back to step 2: Option B = true and true = true. So isMatch('a', 'a*') = true. Step 5: Back to step 1: Option B = true and true = true. Since Option A was false, final result = true. Output: true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Handling '*' with Branching Recursion
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive branching logic for '*' wildcard in regex matching.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the critical part of regex matching: handling the '*' character, which matches zero or more of the preceding element. The intuition is that '*' introduces non-determinism, requiring exploration of multiple possibilities. When encountering a pattern like 'x*' (where 'x' is any character or '.'), we must decide whether to match zero occurrences (skip 'x*') or match at least one occurrence (consume one character from the string and keep 'x*' for potential further matches). This leads to a branching recursion: one branch skips the pattern by moving past 'x*', and another branch consumes a character if it matches 'x'. The algorithm checks if the pattern has at least two characters and the second is '*'; if so, it recursively calls itself with two options: (1) pattern advanced by 2 (zero match), or (2) if the first character matches, string advanced by 1 with pattern unchanged (one-or-more match). This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach, as it generates exponential complexity. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation is O(2^k) where k is the number of '*' branches, but in full context, it contributes to O(2^(n+m)). Space is O(n+m) due to recursion depth. Connection to L0: This subroutine is invoked within the full isMatch function to handle '*' cases. Common mistakes include not checking the first character match before the one-or-more branch, or incorrectly handling empty strings. Real code pattern: if len(p) >= 2 and p[1] == '*': return isMatch(s, p[2:]) or (first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p)).

examples.trace: Assume we are at isMatch('aa', 'a*'). first_match = true ('a' matches 'a'). Since p[1]=='*', branch: Option A: isMatch('aa', '') -> false (s not empty). Option B: first_match and isMatch('a', 'a*') = true and isMatch('a', 'a*'). Recurse: isMatch('a', 'a*'): first_match = true, p[1]=='*', branch: Option A: isMatch('a', '') -> false. Option B: true and isMatch('', 'a*') = true and isMatch('', 'a*'). isMatch('', 'a*'): first_match = false, p[1]=='*', branch: Option A: isMatch('', '') -> true. Option B: false and ... -> false. So isMatch('', 'a*') = true. Back: true and true = true. So isMatch('a', 'a*') = true. Back: true and true = true. Final: true.

Call isMatch('b', 'a*b'). first_match = (s not empty and p[0] in {s[0], '.'}) = ('a' in {'b'}? false) so false. len(p)>=2 and p[1]=='*'? Yes ('a*'). Branch: Option A: isMatch('b', 'b') -> p='b', first_match = ('b' in {'b'}) = true, no '*', so return true and isMatch('', '') = true. Option B: first_match and isMatch(s[1:], p) = false and ... -> false. So isMatch('b', 'a*b') = true (from Option A). Output: true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Character-by-Character Matching
ladders.desc: Implement the basic recursive matching of strings and patterns without wildcards.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the foundational recursive logic for matching strings and patterns character by character, ignoring wildcards like '*' initially. The intuition is to break down the problem into smaller subproblems: check if the first characters match, then recursively match the rest of the string and pattern. This is a classic divide-and-conquer approach. The algorithm proceeds as follows: if the pattern is empty, return whether the string is empty (base case). Otherwise, check if the first character of the string matches the first character of the pattern (direct equality or '.' for any character). If they match, recursively call the function with the remaining strings (s[1:] and p[1:]). This simple recursion handles cases without '*' and sets the stage for adding wildcard handling. Time complexity is O(n+m) in this simplified version, as each recursive call reduces both string and pattern by one character. Space complexity is O(n+m) due to recursion stack. Connection to L1: This core logic is extended in L1 by adding branching for '*'. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings correctly or off-by-one errors in slicing. Real code pattern: def match(s, p): if not p: return not s; first_match = bool(s) and (p[0] == s[0] or p[0] == '.'); return first_match and match(s[1:], p[1:]).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call match('abc', 'abc'). p not empty, first_match = (s not empty and 'a' == 'a') = true. Return true and match('bc', 'bc'). Step 2: Call match('bc', 'bc'). first_match = ('b' == 'b') = true. Return true and match('c', 'c'). Step 3: Call match('c', 'c'). first_match = ('c' == 'c') = true. Return true and match('', ''). Step 4: Call match('', ''). p empty, s empty, return true. Back propagate: true and true and true and true = true. Output: true.

Step 1: Call match('ab', 'ac'). p not empty, first_match = ('a' == 'a') = true. Return true and match('b', 'c'). Step 2: Call match('b', 'c'). first_match = ('b' == 'c') = false. Return false and match(...) = false. Back to step 1: true and false = false. Output: false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Handling '.' Wildcard for Single Character Matching
ladders.desc: Implement matching with '.' which matches any single character.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the '.' wildcard, which matches any single character in the string. The intuition is to extend exact character matching by allowing flexibility: when comparing the first character of the string and pattern, if the pattern character is '.', it should match any non-empty string character. This is a simple enhancement to the core logic from L2. The algorithm modifies the first_match check: instead of requiring exact equality, it accepts a match if the pattern character is '.' or equals the string character. This building block is essential for regex patterns, as '.' provides a basic wildcard capability. Time and space complexity remain O(n+m) for this sub-problem, as it only adds a constant-time check. Connection to L2: This is integrated into the recursive matching by updating the condition for first_match. Common mistakes include not ensuring the string is non-empty when checking '.' match, or confusing '.' with '*'. Real code pattern: first_match = bool(s) and (p[0] == s[0] or p[0] == '.').

examples.trace: Step 1: Call match('cat', 'c.t'). p not empty, first_match = (s not empty and ('c' == 'c' or '.' == 'c')) = true. Return true and match('at', '.t'). Step 2: Call match('at', '.t'). first_match = (s not empty and ('.' == 'a' or '.' == 'a')) = true (since p[0] is '.'). Return true and match('t', 't'). Step 3: Call match('t', 't'). first_match = ('t' == 't') = true. Return true and match('', ''). Step 4: Call match('', ''): true. Back: true and true and true and true = true. Output: true.

Step 1: Call match('ab', '..'). first_match = (s not empty and '.' == 'a') = true. Return true and match('b', '.'). Step 2: Call match('b', '.'). first_match = (s not empty and '.' == 'b') = true. Return true and match('', ''). Step 3: Call match('', ''): true. Back: true and true and true = true. Output: true.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking First Character Match
ladders.desc: Implement a function to check if the first characters of a string and pattern match.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the most basic operation in regex matching: determining if the first character of the string matches the first character of the pattern. The intuition is that this is the atomic step upon which all recursive matching is built. The operation must handle two cases: exact character match or match via '.' wildcard. It also needs to account for empty strings: if the string is empty, there is no first character to match, so the result is false unless the pattern is also empty (handled at a higher level). The algorithm is straightforward: check if the string is non-empty, and if so, compare the first character of the string with the first character of the pattern; if they are equal or the pattern character is '.', return true; otherwise, return false. This operation runs in O(1) time and uses O(1) space. Connection to L3: This operation is used as part of the first_match check in recursive matching. Common mistakes include not checking if the string is empty before accessing s[0], which could cause index errors. Real code pattern: def first_match(s, p): return bool(s) and (p[0] == s[0] or p[0] == '.').

examples.trace: Step: Check first_match('apple', 'a'). s is not empty (True), p[0] is 'a', s[0] is 'a', so 'a' == 'a' is true. Return true.
Step: Check first_match('banana', '.'). s is not empty (True), p[0] is '.', so condition p[0] == '.' is true (or check: '.' matches any char). Return true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion with String Slicing
ladders.desc: Learn to use recursion to process strings by slicing them into smaller parts.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the foundational concept of recursion applied to strings, specifically using slicing to break problems into smaller subproblems. The intuition is that many string problems can be solved by handling the first character and recursively processing the rest. Recursion involves a function calling itself with modified inputs, typically reducing the problem size each time until a base case is reached. For strings, slicing (e.g., s[1:] to get all characters except the first) is a common way to pass the remaining portion. The base case often involves empty strings. This concept is crucial for regex matching, as we recursively match prefixes. The algorithm: define a recursive function that takes a string s; if s is empty, return some result (base case); otherwise, process s[0] and call the function recursively with s[1:]. Time complexity depends on the number of recursive calls, often O(n) for simple recursion without branching. Space complexity is O(n) due to recursion stack. Connection to L4: This foundation is used to implement operations like first_match in a recursive context. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if base case is missing, or incorrect slicing indices. Real code pattern: def process(s): if not s: return True; # base case; do something with s[0]; return process(s[1:]).

examples.input: s = 'hello' to check if all characters are letters
examples.trace: Define function all_letters(s): if s empty, return true; else, check if s[0] is a letter; if yes, return all_letters(s[1:]); else return false. Step 1: Call all_letters('hello'). s not empty, 'h' is letter, return all_letters('ello'). Step 2: Call all_letters('ello'). 'e' is letter, return all_letters('llo'). Step 3: Call all_letters('llo'). 'l' is letter, return all_letters('lo'). Step 4: Call all_letters('lo'). 'l' is letter, return all_letters('o'). Step 5: Call all_letters('o'). 'o' is letter, return all_letters(''). Step 6: Call all_letters(''): s empty, return true. Back propagate: true. Output: true.

examples.input: s = '123a' to check if all characters are digits
examples.trace: Define function all_digits(s): if s empty, return true; else, check if s[0] is digit; if yes, return all_digits(s[1:]); else return false. Step 1: Call all_digits('123a'). s not empty, '1' is digit, return all_digits('23a'). Step 2: Call all_digits('23a'). '2' is digit, return all_digits('3a'). Step 3: Call all_digits('3a'). '3' is digit, return all_digits('a'). Step 4: Call all_digits('a'). 'a' is not digit, return false. Back propagate: false. Output: false.

testCases.input: s = 'test' to return last character recursively
s = 'banana' to find first 'a' index recursively
s = 'xyz' to check if all uppercase recursively
s = 'recursion' to return string without first char recursively

approaches.name: Top-Down DP with Memoization O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Add memoization to the brute force recursion to cache results of subproblems, avoiding redundant computations. Suitable for clarity and easier debugging.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Regex Matching with Top-Down DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of regex matching using recursion with memoization to cache subproblem results.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements regex matching where '.' matches any single character and '*' matches zero or more of the preceding element. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems: matching prefixes of the string against prefixes of the pattern. We use recursion to explore all possibilities, but add memoization to avoid exponential time complexity by caching results of (i, j) pairs where i is the string index and j is the pattern index. The base cases handle when we reach the end of either string or pattern. For the recursive case, we check if the current pattern character has a '*' following it. If yes, we have two options: skip the '*' and preceding character (match zero times), or match the current character if it matches and continue with the same pattern (match one or more times). If no '*', we simply match the current characters if they match. The time complexity is O(n*m) where n is string length and m is pattern length, as each subproblem (i, j) is computed once. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the memoization cache plus recursion stack. This approach is clearer than bottom-up DP for understanding the recursive nature of the problem while being efficient. Common mistakes include not handling the '*' operator correctly (especially the zero-match case), off-by-one errors in indices, and forgetting to memoize before returning results. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the memoization framework with the recursive matching logic.

examples.trace:
1. Call match(0,0): i=0, j=0, n=2, m=1
2. Check if j==m: false (j=0, m=1)
3. Check if i==n: false (i=0, n=2)
4. Check if j+1<m and p[j+1]=='*': false (j+1=1, m=1, so j+1 not < m)
5. Check first_match: s[0]='a', p[0]='a', match true
6. Recursively call match(1,1): i=1, j=1
7. Check if j==m: true (j=1, m=1)
8. Check if i==n: false (i=1, n=2)
9. Return false
10. Back to match(0,0): return false

1. Call match(0,0): i=0, j=0, n=2, m=2
2. Check if j==m: false (j=0, m=2)
3. Check if i==n: false (i=0, n=2)
4. Check if j+1<m and p[j+1]=='*': true (j+1=1, p[1]='*')
5. Two options:
   Option A: skip '*' pattern: call match(0,2): i=0, j=2
     - Check if j==m: true (j=2, m=2)
     - Check if i==n: false (i=0, n=2)
     - Return false
   Option B: match current char if possible: first_match = (i<n) and (p[j] in {s[i], '.'}) = true (s[0]='a', p[0]='a')
     - If first_match: call match(1,0): i=1, j=0
       - Check if j==m: false (j=0, m=2)
       - Check if i==n: false (i=1, n=2)
       - Check if j+1<m and p[j+1]=='*': true (j+1=1, p[1]='*')
       - Two options:
         Option B1: skip '*' pattern: call match(1,2): i=1, j=2
           - Check if j==m: true (j=2, m=2)
           - Check if i==n: false (i=1, n=2)
           - Return false
         Option B2: match current char: first_match = true (s[1]='a', p[0]='a')
           - Call match(2,0): i=2, j=0
             - Check if j==m: false (j=0, m=2)
             - Check if i==n: true (i=2, n=2)
             - Check if j+1<m and p[j+1]=='*': true (j+1=1, p[1]='*')
             - Option: skip '*' pattern: call match(2,2): i=2, j=2
               - Check if j==m: true (j=2, m=2)
               - Check if i==n: true (i=2, n=2)
               - Return true
             - Return true
           - Return true
       - Return true
     - Return true
6. Return true

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Matching Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that determines if string[i:] matches pattern[j:], with memoization to cache results.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the solution: a recursive function match(i, j) that returns true if the substring s[i:] matches the subpattern p[j:]. The intuition is that regex matching has optimal substructure - the match of the entire strings depends on matching prefixes. We use memoization (a dictionary or 2D array) to store results of (i, j) pairs to avoid recomputation. The function first checks if (i, j) is already computed and returns the cached value. Base cases: if j reaches the end of pattern, return whether i also reached end of string (empty pattern matches empty string). If i reaches end of string, we still need to check if remaining pattern can match empty string (e.g., patterns like 'a*b*c*'). The recursive case handles two scenarios: when the next pattern character is '*', we have the choice to match zero occurrences (skip the '*' and its preceding character) or match one occurrence if the current characters match. When there's no '*', we simply match current characters if they match and recurse. The time complexity for this function with memoization is O(n*m) as each (i, j) pair is computed once. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the memo cache. Connection to L0: this function is called by the main solution with i=0, j=0. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling, not checking bounds before accessing string/pattern indices, and forgetting to store results in memo before returning.

examples.trace:
1. Check memo for (0,0): not found
2. Check if j==len(p): false (j=0, len=1)
3. Check if i==len(s): false (i=0, len=1)
4. Check if j+1<len(p) and p[j+1]=='*': false (j+1=1, len=1)
5. first_match = (i<len(s)) and (p[j] in {s[i], '.'}) = true (s[0]='a', p[0]='.' matches any)
6. Recursively call match(1,1): i=1, j=1
   - Check memo for (1,1): not found
   - Check if j==len(p): true (j=1, len=1)
   - Check if i==len(s): true (i=1, len=1)
   - Return true
   - Store memo[(1,1)]=true
7. Store memo[(0,0)]=true
8. Return true

1. Check memo for (0,0): not found
2. Check if j==len(p): false (j=0, len=2)
3. Check if i==len(s): true (i=0, len=0)
4. Check if j+1<len(p) and p[j+1]=='*': true (j+1=1, p[1]='*')
5. Two options:
   Option A: skip '*' pattern: call match(0,2): i=0, j=2
     - Check memo for (0,2): not found
     - Check if j==len(p): true (j=2, len=2)
     - Check if i==len(s): true (i=0, len=0)
     - Return true
     - Store memo[(0,2)]=true
   Option B: match current char: first_match = (i<len(s)) and (p[j] in {s[i], '.'}) = false (i=0, len=0, so i<len(s) false)
     - Skip this option
6. Return true (from option A)
7. Store memo[(0,0)]=true
8. Return true

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling '*' Operator in Regex Matching
ladders.desc: Implement the logic for matching patterns containing '*' which matches zero or more of the preceding element.
ladders.explanation: The '*' operator is the most complex part of regex matching because it introduces non-determinism. The intuition is that when we encounter a pattern character followed by '*', we have two choices: 1) Match zero occurrences of the preceding character (skip both the character and '*'), or 2) Match one occurrence if the current string character matches the preceding pattern character, then continue trying to match more occurrences with the same pattern. This creates a branching recursion. Formally, if p[j+1] == '*', then match(i, j) = match(i, j+2) [skip case] OR (first_match AND match(i+1, j)) [match case]. The skip case handles patterns like 'a*' matching empty string. The match case handles one or more repetitions. The time complexity without memoization would be exponential due to these branches, but with memoization it becomes O(n*m). Connection to L1: this logic is part of the recursive function's main case. Common mistakes include: only implementing the match case and forgetting the skip case, incorrectly handling the case where i is at the end of string (should still allow skip case), and not checking that j+1 is within bounds before accessing p[j+1]. Real code pattern: check if j+1 < m and p[j+1] == '*', then return dp(i, j+2) or (first_match and dp(i+1, j)).

examples.trace:
Handling '*' at j=0 (p[1]='*'):
Option 1 (skip): match(0,2) - skip 'a*', check if '' matches '' -> true
Option 2 (match): first_match = s[0] matches p[0] ('a' matches 'a') = true, then match(1,0)
  - Now at i=1, j=0, again p[1]='*'
  Option 2.1 (skip): match(1,2) - check if 'aa' matches '' -> false
  Option 2.2 (match): first_match = s[1] matches p[0] = true, then match(2,0)
    - At i=2, j=0, again p[1]='*'
    Option 2.2.1 (skip): match(2,2) - check if 'a' matches '' -> false
    Option 2.2.2 (match): first_match = s[2] matches p[0] = true, then match(3,0)
      - At i=3, j=0, i at end, p[1]='*'
      Option (skip): match(3,2) - both at end -> true
    Return true
  Return true
Return true

Handling '*' at j=0 (p[1]='*'):
Option 1 (skip): match(0,2) - skip 'a*', check if 'ab' matches 'b' -> false (first char 'a' doesn't match 'b')
Option 2 (match): first_match = s[0] matches p[0] ('a' matches 'a') = true, then match(1,0)
  - At i=1, j=0, again p[1]='*'
  Option 2.1 (skip): match(1,2) - skip 'a*', check if 'b' matches 'b' -> true
  Option 2.2 (match): first_match = s[1] matches p[0] ('b' matches 'a') = false, skip
  Return true
Return true

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive String Matching Without '*'
ladders.desc: Implement recursive matching for patterns that don't contain '*' (only '.' and literal characters).
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the simpler case of regex matching where the pattern contains only literal characters and '.' (which matches any single character), but no '*' operators. The intuition is straightforward: we match the strings character by character recursively. If the current pattern character is '.' or matches the current string character, we move to the next positions in both string and pattern. If we reach the end of both simultaneously, it's a match. If we reach the end of one before the other, it's not a match. The algorithm: function match(i, j): if j == len(p): return i == len(s). If i == len(s): return false (since no '*' to match empty). Otherwise, check if p[j] == '.' or p[j] == s[i]. If true, return match(i+1, j+1). Else return false. Time complexity is O(min(n,m)) in the worst case where we traverse to the end of the shorter string. Space complexity is O(min(n,m)) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: this is the base case when there's no '*' following the current pattern character. Common mistakes include: not checking if i is within bounds before accessing s[i], and incorrect handling of the '.' character. Real code pattern: if p[j] in {s[i], '.'}: return match(i+1, j+1).

examples.trace:
1. i=0, j=0: p[0]='a', s[0]='a' match -> call match(1,1)
2. i=1, j=1: p[1]='.', matches any -> call match(2,2)
3. i=2, j=2: p[2]='c', s[2]='c' match -> call match(3,3)
4. i=3, j=3: j==len(p)=3 true, i==len(s)=3 true -> return true

1. i=0, j=0: p[0]='a', s[0]='a' match -> call match(1,1)
2. i=1, j=1: p[1]='b', s[1]='b' match -> call match(2,2)
3. i=2, j=2: j==len(p)=3 false, i==len(s)=2 true -> return false

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Matching with '.' Wildcard
ladders.desc: Implement the basic character comparison that supports '.' matching any single character.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation at the heart of regex matching: checking if a pattern character matches a string character. The pattern character can be either a literal character (like 'a', 'b', etc.) or the special wildcard '.' which matches any single character. The intuition is simple: if the pattern character is '.', it automatically matches whatever the current string character is (as long as we haven't reached the end of the string). If it's a literal character, it only matches if it's exactly equal to the current string character. Formally: match = (i < len(s)) and (p[j] == '.' or p[j] == s[i]). We must check i < len(s) to avoid index out of bounds when the string is exhausted. This operation has O(1) time and space complexity. Connection to L3: this operation is used in each step of the recursive matching when there's no '*' involved. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check that i is within bounds before accessing s[i], and misunderstanding that '.' matches exactly one character (not zero or multiple). Real code pattern: first_match = i < len(s) and (p[j] == '.' or p[j] == s[i]).

examples.trace:
1. Check i < len(s): 0 < 1 true
2. Check p[j] == '.' or p[j] == s[i]: p[0]='.' matches any -> true
3. Return true

1. Check i < len(s): 0 < 0 false
2. Return false (even though p[j]='.', we can't match because no character in s)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Understand how to safely access characters in strings using indices and check bounds.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves working with string indices to access individual characters while ensuring we don't go out of bounds. Strings are typically zero-indexed: the first character is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. The length of a string tells us how many characters it contains. To safely access a character at index i, we must first verify that i is within the valid range: 0 <= i < len(string). If i equals len(string), we're at the position just past the last character (often representing the end of the string). Attempting to access string[i] when i is out of bounds will cause an error. The intuition is simple: before accessing any array or string element, always check if the index is valid. This is especially important in recursive algorithms where indices change dynamically. Time complexity for bounds checking is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L4: bounds checking is essential before comparing characters in the matching operation. Common mistakes include: assuming indices start at 1, forgetting to check upper bound, or confusing string length with last valid index (last valid index is len(string)-1). Real code pattern: if i < len(s): # safe to access s[i].

examples.trace:
1. String 'hello' has length 5
2. Check if i=2 is within bounds: 0 <= 2 < 5 true
3. Access s[2]: character at position 2 is 'l' (indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o')
4. Return 'l'

1. String 'world' has length 5
2. Check if i=5 is within bounds: 0 <= 5 < 5 false (5 is not less than 5)
3. Cannot access s[5] - would cause index error
4. Must handle this case (e.g., return None or handle specially)

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DP Tabulation O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Build a DP table iteratively from base cases up to solve the entire problem. Prefer this for optimal space usage and avoiding recursion overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Regex Matching with Bottom-Up DP Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of regex matching using bottom-up DP table with O(n*m) time and space.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements regex matching where '.' matches any single character and '*' matches zero or more of the preceding element. We use bottom-up dynamic programming with a 2D boolean table dp[i][j] representing whether the first i characters of text match the first j characters of pattern. The intuition is to build solutions from smallest subproblems upward, avoiding recursion overhead while maintaining optimal space usage. We initialize dp[0][0] = true (empty strings match). For each position, we handle three cases: 1) If pattern character is '*', we can either skip the preceding pattern element (dp[i][j-2]) or match current text character if it matches the preceding pattern element. 2) If pattern character is '.' or matches text character, we propagate from diagonal. 3) Otherwise, no match. The algorithm iterates through all i from 0 to n and j from 1 to m, building the complete solution. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is text length and m is pattern length. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the DP table. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete DP table construction. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of '*' with zero matches and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: Initialize dp table with False, set dp[0][0]=True, handle base cases for pattern with '*', then fill table row by row.

examples.trace: n=2, m=1. Initialize dp[3][2] all False. dp[0][0]=True. Fill: i=0,j=1: pattern[0]='a' not '*', dp[0][1]=False. i=1,j=1: text[0]='a', pattern[0]='a', match, dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=2,j=1: text[1]='a', pattern[0]='a', match, dp[2][1]=dp[1][0]=False (since dp[1][0]=False). Final dp[2][1]=False.

n=2, m=2. Initialize dp[3][3]. dp[0][0]=True. Fill: i=0,j=1: pattern[0]='a' not '*', dp[0][1]=False. i=0,j=2: pattern[1]='*', dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True (skip 'a'). i=1,j=1: text[0]='a', pattern[0]='a', match, dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=1,j=2: pattern[1]='*', check if text[0] matches pattern[0]: yes, dp[1][2]=dp[0][2] or dp[1][0]=True or False=True. i=2,j=1: text[1]='a', pattern[0]='a', match, dp[2][1]=dp[1][0]=False. i=2,j=2: pattern[1]='*', text[1] matches pattern[0], dp[2][2]=dp[1][2] or dp[2][0]=True or False=True. Final dp[2][2]=True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Cell Computation for '*' Pattern
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to compute dp[i][j] when pattern character is '*', handling zero or more matches.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the value of dp[i][j] when pattern[j-1] is '*', which is the most complex part of regex matching. The intuition is that '*' allows zero or more repetitions of the preceding character. We handle two cases: 1) Zero matches: skip the preceding pattern element by checking dp[i][j-2]. 2) One or more matches: if the current text character matches the preceding pattern character (or pattern has '.'), we propagate from dp[i-1][j] (matching one more occurrence). The algorithm: if pattern[j-1] == '*', set dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] (zero matches). Then if i > 0 and (text[i-1] == pattern[j-2] or pattern[j-2] == '.'), set dp[i][j] = dp[i][j] or dp[i-1][j] (one more match). Time complexity for this computation is O(1) per cell. Space complexity is part of the overall O(n*m). This connects to L0 as the core logic for filling the DP table. Common mistakes include forgetting to check i > 0 before accessing text[i-1], and incorrect indices for pattern[j-2]. Real code pattern: if pattern[j-1] == '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2]; if i > 0 and (text[i-1] == pattern[j-2] or pattern[j-2] == '.'): dp[i][j] = dp[i][j] or dp[i-1][j].

examples.trace: Given dp table partially filled. pattern[1]='*'. Compute dp[0][2]: First, zero matches: dp[0][2] = dp[0][0] = True. Since i=0, skip one-or-more match check. Result: True.

examples.input: dp[2][2] where text='aa', pattern='a*', i=2, j=2
examples.trace: Given dp table with dp[1][2]=True, dp[2][0]=False. pattern[1]='*'. Compute dp[2][2]: Zero matches: dp[2][2] = dp[2][0] = False. Since i=2>0 and text[1]='a' matches pattern[0]='a', do one-or-more: dp[2][2] = False or dp[1][2] = True. Result: True.

testCases.input: i=1, j=2, text='a', pattern='a*', dp[1][0]=False, dp[0][2]=True
i=1, j=2, text='b', pattern='a*', dp[1][0]=False, dp[0][2]=True
i=2, j=2, text='aa', pattern='a*', dp[2][0]=False, dp[1][2]=True
i=1, j=2, text='a', pattern='.*', dp[1][0]=False, dp[0][2]=True
i=3, j=4, text='aaa', pattern='a*', dp[3][0]=False, dp[2][4]=True
i=1, j=2, text='c', pattern='a*', dp[1][0]=False, dp[0][2]=True
i=2, j=2, text='ab', pattern='.*', dp[2][0]=False, dp[1][2]=True
i=1, j=2, text='', pattern='a*', dp[1][0]=False, dp[0][2]=True

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Transition for Matching Single Characters
ladders.desc: Implement the DP transition when pattern character is not '*', matching '.' or exact characters.
ladders.explanation: This core logic handles the case when pattern[j-1] is not '*', requiring matching of a single character. The intuition is straightforward: if the current text character matches the current pattern character (either exactly or via '.' wildcard), then the match succeeds only if all previous characters also matched. We propagate the match from the diagonal cell dp[i-1][j-1]. The algorithm: if pattern[j-1] != '*', then if i > 0 and (text[i-1] == pattern[j-1] or pattern[j-1] == '.'), set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; otherwise, dp[i][j] = False. Time complexity is O(1) per cell. Space complexity is part of the overall DP table. This connects to L1 as it handles the non-'*' cases in the DP table. Common mistakes include forgetting to check i > 0 before accessing text[i-1], and not handling '.' correctly. Real code pattern: if pattern[j-1] != '*': if i > 0 and (text[i-1] == pattern[j-1] or pattern[j-1] == '.'): dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] else dp[i][j] = False.

examples.input: dp[1][1] where text='a', pattern='a', i=1, j=1
examples.trace: Given dp[0][0]=True. pattern[0]='a' not '*'. i=1>0 and text[0]='a' matches pattern[0]='a'. So dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] = True.

examples.input: dp[1][1] where text='b', pattern='a', i=1, j=1
examples.trace: Given dp[0][0]=True. pattern[0]='a' not '*'. i=1>0 but text[0]='b' does not match pattern[0]='a'. So dp[1][1] = False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Initializing DP Base Cases
ladders.desc: Set up the DP table with base cases for empty strings and patterns with '*'.
ladders.explanation: This building block initializes the DP table before filling the main cells. The intuition is that we need to establish truth values for boundary conditions: empty text with empty pattern matches (dp[0][0]=True), and empty text with patterns that can match zero characters (via '*') might also match. The algorithm: Create a 2D table dp of size (n+1) x (m+1) initialized to False. Set dp[0][0] = True. Then for j from 1 to m: if pattern[j-1] == '*', set dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-2] (since empty text can match pattern with '*' by skipping the preceding element). Time complexity is O(m) for this step. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the table. This connects to L2 by providing the initialized table for further computations. Common mistakes include not initializing all cells to False, or incorrect handling of dp[0][j] for non-'*' patterns. Real code pattern: dp = [[False]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; dp[0][0] = True; for j in range(1, m+1): if pattern[j-1] == '*': dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-2].

examples.trace: Initialize dp[1][3] all False. Set dp[0][0]=True. j=1: pattern[0]='a' not '*', dp[0][1]=False. j=2: pattern[1]='*', dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True.

Initialize dp[1][5] all False. dp[0][0]=True. j=1: pattern[0]='a' not '*', dp[0][1]=False. j=2: pattern[1]='*', dp[0][2]=dp[0][0]=True. j=3: pattern[2]='b' not '*', dp[0][3]=False. j=4: pattern[3]='*', dp[0][4]=dp[0][2]=True.

testCases.expected: dp[0][0]=True, dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=True, dp[0][3]=False, dp[0][4]=True, dp[0][5]=False
dp[0][0]=True, dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=True, dp[0][3]=False, dp[0][4]=True, dp[0][5]=False, dp[0][6]=True

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Matching with Wildcards
ladders.desc: Check if a single text character matches a single pattern character, handling '.' wildcard.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation determines whether a given text character matches a given pattern character, where '.' in the pattern matches any single character. The intuition is simple: if the pattern character is '.', it matches any text character; otherwise, it must match exactly. The algorithm: function match(char_text, char_pattern): return True if char_pattern == '.' or char_text == char_pattern, else False. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used in DP transitions for non-'*' cases. Common mistakes include not handling '.' correctly or comparing characters incorrectly. Real code pattern: def match(t, p): return p == '.' or t == p.

examples.trace: char_pattern is 'a', not '.', so check equality: 'a' == 'a' is True.
char_pattern is '.', which matches any character, so return True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding '*' as Zero or More Repetitions
ladders.desc: Learn how the '*' metacharacter works in regex, matching zero or more of the preceding element.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept explains the '*' operator in regular expressions, which is crucial for the problem. The intuition: '*' does not stand alone; it modifies the preceding character (or group) to match zero or more occurrences of that character. For example, 'a*' matches '', 'a', 'aa', 'aaa', etc. Key points: 1) Zero matches: 'a*' can match empty string. 2) Multiple matches: 'a*' can match one or more 'a's. 3) Precedence: '*' applies to the immediate preceding element. The algorithm: To check if a string matches a pattern with '*', consider both skipping the preceding element (zero matches) and matching it repeatedly (one or more matches). Time and space analysis not applicable here as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 as it underpins the logic for handling '*' in DP. Common mistakes include thinking '*' matches any character or forgetting it needs a preceding element. Real code pattern: Conceptual understanding; no code yet.

examples.trace: 'a*' means zero or more 'a's. Text '' has zero 'a's, so it matches by zero matches.
'a*' means zero or more 'a's. Text 'aaa' has three 'a's, so it matches by multiple matches.
